fuzzy_prd:
We need a stream pipeline engine that lets developers compose a finite sequence of fallible values through declarative transformation stages and collect the result. Each element in a source sequence is either a successful integer item or an error carrying an integer code. Stages can map values, filter them by predicate, combine filter and map in one step, take or skip leading elements (counting only successful items, not errors), stop or skip while a predicate holds, rewrite error codes, chain fallible transforms on success or on any outcome, recover from errors by substituting a value, and combine two sequences by pairing their elements or by racing to whichever produces first. There is also a batching stage that groups items into fixed-size chunks. The terminal collectors either gather all items into a list, fold items into a single accumulator, or simply drain the sequence. A separate flatten operation concatenates groups together. Handle errors the way the standard transform chain handles them — when an error arrives it stops the collecting sink and that error code is reported, unless a recovery stage intercepts it.

A couple small clarifications from the questions that came in. On the output side, successful items are reported as 'item=<value>
' lines. If an error makes it through and isn’t recovered, collection stops and that is reported as 'error=<code>
'. If the collect completes normally, it produces 'end
'. The drain sink follows that same basic reporting shape for termination, so it reports errors as 'error=<code>
' and normal completion as 'end
'. For fold_sum, success comes back as 'value=<result>
' and failure still comes back as 'error=<code>
'.

Also, for take(n) and skip(n), the count only advances on SUCCESSFUL items. Error outcomes should still be forwarded, but they do NOT count against the limit. So the intended behavior is that take(2) on [1,err(5),2,3] yields items 1 and 2, the error is forwarded and does not count.

And just to pin down the collector formats for the later features: the zip sink (feature5_1) emits paired results as 'pair=<a>,<b>
' for each pair, then 'end
'. The select/race sink (feature5_2) reports winners as 'item=<value>
' and then 'end
'. The chunks sink (feature6) reports each batch as 'chunk=<v1>,<v2>,...
' and then 'end
'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: trait Stream { type Item; type Error; fn poll(&mut self) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>, Self::Error>; }

trigger_keywords: how does or_else recover from errors
what is the recovery stage behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: or_else converts each error outcome into a successful item carrying the error code value (cast to i32). Successful items pass through unchanged. This allows the collect sink to see the error-code as a regular item rather than stopping.

trigger_keywords: what is the concat flatten operation format
how is the input structured for flatten
what is the shape of a concat request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The concat request uses a 'concat' top-level field (not 'source'+'pipeline'+'sink'). Its elements are arrays of integers (successful groups) or {'err':n} error outcomes. Groups are flattened end to end. An error element stops flattening and emits 'error=<code>\n'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Handle errors the way the standard transform chain handles them — when an error arrives it stops the collecting sink
context_pointers.actual_meaning: In the futures 0.1 Stream trait, an Err result from poll() propagates upward and stops the stream. The collect() sink stops and returns the error. The fold() sink similarly propagates the first error. Only or_else can intercept errors in the pipeline.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. A request has 'source', optional 'pipeline', and a 'sink'; or a 'concat' array for flatten; or a 'combine' with left/right for two-input operations.

feature1_map.json.description: A finite source stream of integer items is transformed element by element by a function that adds a fixed constant to each item; the resulting items are gathered in arrival order. Each surviving item is emitted on its own line followed by a terminator line.

feature2_1_filter.json.description: Elements of an integer source stream are passed through a predicate; only items satisfying the predicate (here, even values) are kept, in order. Kept items are emitted one per line followed by a terminator line.

feature2_2_filter_map.json.description: Each element of an integer source stream is passed to a function that may both drop it and transform it: items not matching the condition (here, odd values) are discarded, while matching items (even values) are replaced by the item plus a fixed constant. Surviving transformed items are emitted in order.

feature2_3_take.json.description: A prefix limiter forwards at most a fixed number of SUCCESSFUL items from the source and then ends the stream. Error outcomes encountered before the limit is reached are forwarded but do not count toward the limit; reaching the count of successful items terminates the stream.

feature2_4_skip.json.description: A prefix dropper discards a fixed number of leading SUCCESSFUL items and then forwards the remainder of the stream. Error outcomes are forwarded unchanged and are not counted against the number of items to skip.

feature2_5_take_while.json.description: Elements are forwarded from the start of the stream as long as a predicate holds (here, while the item is below a threshold); the first item that fails the predicate ends the stream and is not emitted.

feature2_6_skip_while.json.description: Leading elements are discarded as long as a predicate holds (here, while the item is odd); once the predicate first fails, that element and all subsequent elements are forwarded unchanged.

feature3_1_fold.json.description: All successful items of the stream are combined left to right into a single accumulator using addition, starting from a supplied initial value; the final accumulated value is emitted. If the stream yields an error outcome before completing, the aggregation stops and that error is reported instead.

feature3_2_concat.json.description: A stream whose elements are themselves lists is flattened by appending every list end to end into one sequence, which is then emitted item by item. An empty stream yields an empty sequence. If any element is an error outcome, flattening stops and that error is reported.

cases.expected_output:
item=1
item=2
item=3
item=4
item=5
item=6
item=7
item=8
item=9
end

feature4_1_map_err.json.description: A transform applied only to error outcomes rewrites each error code by adding a fixed constant, while successful items pass through unchanged. When the gathered stream reaches an error, the rewritten error code is reported.

feature4_2_and_then.json.description: Each successful item is passed to a fallible transform whose own outcome replaces the item. In the succeeding form the transform adds a constant and yields a new item; in the failing form the transform converts the item into an error outcome carrying that item's value, which stops the gathered stream.

feature4_3_then.json.description: Every outcome of the stream, whether a successful item or an error, is passed to a function that produces the next outcome. Here the function maps successful items by adding a constant; the resulting items are gathered in order.

feature4_4_or_else.json.description: A recovery transform applied only to error outcomes turns each error into a successful item carrying the error's code, while existing successful items pass through unchanged. The gathered stream therefore completes with items in place of the former errors.

feature5_1_zip.json.description: Two source streams are advanced in lockstep, pairing the first item of one with the first of the other, the second with the second, and so on. Pairing stops as soon as either stream ends, so the result length matches the shorter stream. If either stream yields an error outcome, that error is reported instead.

feature5_2_select.json.description: Two source streams are interleaved by alternating between them: take one item from the first, then one from the second, and continue alternating. When one stream is exhausted, the remaining items of the other are emitted in order until it too is exhausted.

cases.expected_output:
item=1
item=4
item=2
item=5
item=3
item=6
end

feature6_chunks.json.description: Successive successful items are batched into fixed-size groups; each full group is emitted as soon as it fills, and any trailing partial group is emitted when the stream ends. Each emitted group lists its items in order. If the stream yields an error outcome, any already-buffered partial group is emitted first and then the error is reported.
